coredata.dc:title: Oyster-inspired carbon dots-functionalized silica and dialdehyde chitosan to fabricate a soy protein adhesive with high strength, mildew resistance, and long-term water resistance
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shiqing
Li, Xinyi
Bai, Mingyang
Shi, Sheldon Q.
Aladejana, John Tosin
Cao, Jinfeng
Li, Jianzhang
coredata.dc:description: Developing multifunctional adhesives with exceptional cold-pressing strength, water resistance, toughness, and mildew resistance remains challenging. Herein, inspired by oysters, a multifunctional organic-inorganic hybrid soybean meal (SM)-based adhesive was fabricated by incorporating amino-modified carbon dots functionalized silica nanoparticles (CDs@SiO2) and dialdehyde chitosan (DCS) into SM matrix. DCS effectively enhanced the interface interactions of organic-inorganic phases and the rigid nanofillers CDs@SiO2 uniformly dispersed in the SM matrix, which provided energy dissipation to improve the adhesive's toughness. Owing to the stiff skeleton structure and enhanced crosslinking density, the crosslinker-modified SM (MSM)/DCS/CDs@SiO2–2 wood adhesive exhibited outstanding cold-pressing strength (0.74 MPa), wet shear strength (1.36 MPa), and long-term water resistance (49 d). Additionally, the resultant adhesive showed superior antimildew and antibacterial properties benefiting from the introduction of DCS. Intriguingly, the fluorescent properties endowed by carbon dots further broadened the application of adhesives for realizing security testing. This study opens a new pathway for the synthesis of multifunctional biomass adhesives in industrial and household applications.

head.ce:title: Oyster-inspired carbon dots-functionalized silica and dialdehyde chitosan to fabricate a soy protein adhesive with high strength, mildew resistance, and long-term water resistance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing multifunctional adhesives with exceptional cold-pressing strength, water resistance, toughness, and mildew resistance remains challenging. Herein, inspired by oysters, a multifunctional organic-inorganic hybrid soybean meal (SM)-based adhesive was fabricated by incorporating amino-modified carbon dots functionalized silica nanoparticles (CDs@SiO2) and dialdehyde chitosan (DCS) into SM matrix. DCS effectively enhanced the interface interactions of organic-inorganic phases and the rigid nanofillers CDs@SiO2 uniformly dispersed in the SM matrix, which provided energy dissipation to improve the adhesive's toughness. Owing to the stiff skeleton structure and enhanced crosslinking density, the crosslinker-modified SM (MSM)/DCS/CDs@SiO2–2 wood adhesive exhibited outstanding cold-pressing strength (0.74 MPa), wet shear strength (1.36 MPa), and long-term water resistance (49 d). Additionally, the resultant adhesive showed superior antimildew and antibacterial properties benefiting from the introduction of DCS. Intriguingly, the fluorescent properties endowed by carbon dots further broadened the application of adhesives for realizing security testing. This study opens a new pathway for the synthesis of multifunctional biomass adhesives in industrial and household applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The need for developing bio-based adhesives to manufacture wood-based materials is compelling as nonrenewable petrochemical-derived synthetic adhesives could pose a threat to environment and human health (Hadei et al., 2019; Zhao, Liu, et al., 2022). Naturally derived soybean meal (SM) possesses high reliability, abundant reserves, convenient processing, and low cost, which has been widely concerned in the development of eco-friendly wood adhesives (Xu et al., 2019). However, the pristine SM adhesive is not resistant to water and prone to microbial attack since soy protein contains large number of hydrophilic groups and nutrients, severely limiting its industrial application. Previous studies showed that protein modification (Averina et al., 2021; Li, Fuxiao, et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2021), chemical crosslinking (Ghahri et al., 2022; Mousavi et al., 2021; Zeng, Xu, et al., 2021), and biomimetic design (Cheng et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2022; Pang et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2022), have been employed to address the drawbacks of protein adhesives. Among these strategies, the enhanced water resistance of the protein adhesives can be achieved. However, the adhesives still exhibit poor cold-pressing properties that easily make pre-assembled wood substrates dislocate during the transportation before hot pressing, which greatly limits their industrial use. Additionally, large dosages of crosslinkers cause inadequate toughness and mildew resistance of the adhesives. Thus, there is an urgent need to create a simultaneously high-strength, superior cold-pressing, tough, and mildew-resistant wood adhesive.

Reef-building oysters represent an essential “inorganic” adhesion strategy for marine molluscs by producing biomineralized organic-inorganic adhesives. The adhesive material generated by oysters is comprised of proteins, polysaccharides, phospholipids, and an inorganic component of CaCO3, giving rise to the cohesion and surface adhesive bonding together into reef communities, even in the presence of water (Alberts et al., 2015; Li, Jia, et al., 2018; Metzler et al., 2016). Recent works reported that organic protein component as a soft phase can provide binding sites and inorganic nanofillers as a hard phase can tailor the network structure and crosslinking mechanisms in polymer/inorganic system, which could reinforce adhesives' mechanical toughness and adhesion strength (Lei et al., 2020; Li, Jia, et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2018). These advantages are attributed to the strong interface interactions between inorganic nanofillers and organic components (Mao et al., 2016). Among the various types of nanomaterials, including carbon nanotube (Sadare et al., 2020), montmorillonite (Sun et al., 2021), and silica nanoparticle (SiO2) (Zhao, Hao, et al., 2022), SiO2 has recently attracted the most research attention because of its rich surface hydroxyl groups, uniform structure, and strong adsorption capacity (Motealleh & Kehr, 2017). It serves as the reversible physical crosslinking point to dissipate energy, thereby enhancing the mechanical robustness during deformation (Baik et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2021). However, the poor dispersion and interface compatibility of nano-SiO2 led to the weak adhesion of the constructed inorganic-organic protein-based adhesives under wet conditions (Xu et al., 2011). Hence, the design and development of an organic-inorganic hybrid system augmented with SiO2 nanophase in protein matrix is truly challenging.

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of emerging fluorescent nano-particles (2–10 nm), have gained extensive attention because of their outstanding water dispersibility, less cytotoxicity, easy preparation, and fluorescent properties (Cao et al., 2012; Cui et al., 2021; Jian et al., 2020). CDs' surface is rich in functional groups, allowing them to stably disperse in water and easily interact with other moieties (Navidfar et al., 2022). Thus, the existence of numerous functional groups by CDs compounding with SiO2 promotes the homogeneous dispersion of SiO2 in the protein matrix. Additionally, it is interesting to note that the organic fraction phospholipids, as unique glue molecules being prominent in adhesive, promote bonding contacts that make oyster effectively attach to each other and aggregate into large communities (Alberts et al., 2015; Metzler et al., 2016). The organic glue molecules filling in the interface of organic-inorganic phases play a key role in constructing a stable stiff structure and regulating the adhesive's performance (Alberts et al., 2015; Li et al., 2021). This is due to their ability to bind the nanofillers tightly to the polymeric matrix, thereby making strong interfacial contacts between the nanofillers and matrix (Hu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021). Therefore, the unique biomimetic structure and molecular design are paramount for preparing high-performance adhesives.

Chitosan (CS) with massive hydroxyl and amino groups is the second most abundant polysaccharide biopolymers derived from large fishery waste (Andreica et al., 2023; Asere et al., 2019), which is non-toxic, biodegradable, biocompatible, and possesses antimicrobial properties. Mimicking the oyster adhesive mechanism, CS could be used as an organic glue molecule in organic-inorganic hybrid system to successfully fabricate high water-resistant materials (Han et al., 2020). However, its water-insoluble nature under neutral conditions limits the applications. Dialdehyde chitosan (DCS) with aldehyde groups can be obtained by oxidizing the C2 and C3 position of CS (Wang et al., 2020). It has water-soluble property and highly reactive groups, which can generate sufficient interacting cites with polymer matrix to form a stable crosslinked structure. Therefore, it is expected that DCS could act as an effective glue molecule, which closely connects inorganic nanofillers and organic protein matrix via the Schiff base reaction, promoting strong interface interactions and improving the performance for biomimetic adhesives.

Herein, inspired by oysters, a simple but ingenious approach was developed to fabricate a high-performance organic-inorganic hybrid adhesive by incorporating amino-modified carbon dots functionalized silica nanoparticles (CDs@SiO2) and dialdehyde chitosan (DCS) into SM matrix (Scheme 1 ). We hypothesized that the highly reactive aldehyde groups of DCS and excellent dispersion stability of CDs@SiO2 might play a crucial role in the improvement of adhesive's adhesion strength, toughness and long-term water resistance. The physicochemical and morphology of DCS and CDs@SiO2 were characterized. The cold-pressing strength, wet shear strength, toughness and long-term water resistance of resultant protein adhesive were studied. Additionally, the antibacterial and antimildew properties of the adhesive were evaluated. This strategy greatly enriches the biomimetic design idea and possesses great potential for developing robust and multifunctional biomass soy protein adhesives.

Chitosan (CS) (≥95 % deacetylated), ethylene glycol diglycidyl ether (EGDE) (epoxy value: ≥0.7), sodium periodate, branched polyethylenimine (PEI, Mn = 1.0 × 104 g/mol), nano-silica (SiO2), glycerol, and citric acid (CA) were supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid was purchased from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). SM (protein content: 48–52 %) was supplied by Futian Zhengda Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Binzhou, China). Poplar veneers (thickness: 1.5 mm) were purchased from Wen'an (Hebei, China). Absolute ethanol was obtained from Tianjin Dingshengxin Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

DCS was synthesized from chitosan and sodium periodate via a one-step method according to our previous method (Chen et al., 2023). Briefly, 6 g of CS and 300 mL of 0.1 M acetic acid solution were mixed well for 3 h. Then 0.3 M sodium periodate (mass ratio = 1, according to the chitosan content) was added into the mixture. The reaction was continued at 40 °C for 3 h under a dark environment. The mixture was filtered and then the precipitate was obtained by adding absolute ethanol. The product was washed with deionized water (DW) and subsequently dried in an oven at 40 °C.

CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method (Wang et al., 2019). First, 3 g of PEI and 1 g of CA were dissolved in 25 mL of DW. The mixed solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling the autoclave, the obtained reactant was dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) = 3500 Da) with DW for 2 d and subsequently freeze-dried to obtain light yellow CDs.

EGDE was used as a green crosslinker. Pure SM adhesive (SM) and EGDE-modified SM adhesive (MSM) were used as controls. The various mass ratios of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids (mass ratios = 1:1, 1:2, and 2:1) solutions were fabricated by adding CDs (1 g, 0.5 g, and 1 g) and SiO2 (1 g, 1 g, and 0.5 g) into DW (72 g), respectively, with stir for 2 h and then sonicated for 2 h, allowing the CDs and SiO2 to mix well. Then DCS, EGDE, and SM were added into various mass ratios of CDs@SiO2 solution and stirred continuously for 30 min to prepare MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2-1, MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2-2, and MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2-3 adhesives, respectively. The various adhesives mentioned above are presented in Table 1 .

SM (2.5 g) and glycerol (1 g) were added to DW (47 g) and stirred at 80 °C for 1 h. The mixture was ultrasonically treated for 30 min to obtain supernatant solution for further use. The CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids solution was fabricated by adding CDs (0.045 g) and SiO2 (0.09 g) into DW (17 g). Then CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids solution, EGDE (0.18 g), and DCS (0.27 g) were added into the supernatant solution. After continuously stirring at 80 °C for 20 min, the film-forming solution was placed in a vacuum oven at 40 °C for 24 h. The MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2-2 film was finally conditioned in a chamber with 43 ± 2 % RH at 25 °C for 48 h to test fluorescence performance.

Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of the samples (the adhesive was completely cured at 120 °C) were collected via a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Nicolet Instrument Corporation, USA) with the scan ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded on a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer within a range of 800–200 nm. The zeta potentials of the samples were tested through a nanoscale potentiometer (ZS ZEN3600, Malvern, UK). The crystal structure of the samples was collected on an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation within a range of 5°–60° at a rate of 2 °C/min. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected via an Escalab 250XI spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) equipped with Al Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of the samples were recorded on an STA 449 F5 Jupiter (NETZSCH, Germany) with a heat speed of 10 °C/min under N2. The molecular weight of CS and DCS was tested using a gel permeation chromatography (GPC, PL-GPC50). The parameters were used as follows: mobile phase, CS: 50 mM acetic acid +100 mM sodium nitrate buffer flow, DCS: 100 mM sodium nitrate buffer flow, 1.0 mL/min; columns, 2 × PLgel 8 μm, aquagel-OH Mixed-M, 7.5 × 300 mm; injection volume, 100 μL; temperature, 40 °C. Liquid-state 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra of CS (small amount of trifluoroacetic acid‑d (isotopic) in D2O) and DCS (D2O) were conducted on a Bruker 400 MHz NMR Spectrometer. Solid-state 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (13C NMR) spectra of cured adhesives were carried out on a Bruker 400 MHz spectrometer. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the samples were obtained using a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (JEM-F200) at 200 kV. The dilute suspensions of CDs, SiO2, and CDs@SiO2 were sonicated for 5 min and then deposited onto an ultra-thin carbon support grid to dry before TEM test. The surface and cross-sectional morphologies of the cured adhesives were observed using atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Bruker Dimension Icon) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (ZEISS, Germany), respectively. The fractured cross-sections of the cured adhesives were sputter-coated with gold prior to examining it under the SEM microscope. The elemental composition of the cured adhesive was obtained by energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS).

The various adhesives were bonded on wood substrates using an adhesive density of 190–200 g/m2 on the adhesive area (25 mm × 25 mm), and then clamped with dovetail clips under ambient temperature for 30 min. The adhesives' cold-pressing strength was performed by lap shear strength test.

Three-layer plywood was prepared using an adhesive density of 190–200 g/m2 (single side). Then plywood was hot-pressed at 120 °C and 1.0 MPa for 6 min. Plywood was finally placed under ambient temperature for 1 d.

The China National Standard (GB/T 9846-2015) was applied to measure the bonding strength of three-layer plywood (Type II). The specimens (Scheme 2 ) were cut from plywood and immersed in water at 63 ± 3 °C for 3 h. Prior to measurement, the samples were conditioned under ambient temperature for 10 min.

The area of resultant force-distance curves of the wet strength measurement was applied to calculate adhesives' debonding work, as shown in Formula (3). Additionally, the various liquid adhesives were coated onto glass slide and then cured at 120 °C for 2 h to observe their surface morphology. Both the debonding work and surface morphology of the various adhesives were used to assess the adhesives' toughness.
(3)

where W (J) is work, F (N) is force, and S (m) is distance.
The liquid adhesives and cured adhesives were prepared and put in a chamber (30 °C, 99 % RH) for 30 d. The fungal growth of adhesives was observed to assess the antimildew properties.

The antibacterial properties of the fresh liquid adhesives against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) bacterial strains were assessed by the inhibition zone method. First, the bacteria solution was diluted to 106 CFU/mL, 100 μL of bacteria solution was then absorbed and spread evenly on Luria-Bertani solid medium. A hole (diameter: 10 mm) was made in a solid agar plate, the fresh adhesive that had been sterilized by exposing to UV light was then filled into the hole. The petri dish was finally incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to determine the inhibition zone diameter.

The fluorescence spectra of the samples (excitation wavelength at 365 nm) were recorded with an FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The adhesive layer and film were observed under 365 nm ultraviolet light.

The MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2-2 adhesive was bonded on glass, Al, and steel substrates using an adhesive density of 190–200 g/m2 on the adhesive area (25 mm × 25 mm), were cured in the oven at 120 °C for 2 h and applied to test the lap shear strength. Additionally, the wood and steel specimens withstood a weight of 500 g under water were used to test long-term water resistance.

The DCS was fabricated by the oxidation of CS with periodate (Fig. 1a), which is soluble under neutral conditions (Fig. 1b) and could be expected to provide numerous functional groups reacting with polymeric matrix. The FTIR, 1H NMR, and XPS were applied to investigate the chemical structure of CS and DCS. From Fig. 1c, CS showed main peaks at 1647, 1590, and 1076–987 cm−1, corresponding to CO stretching (amide I), NH bending (amide II), and C-O-C stretching of polysaccharide chain, respectively (Ahmed & Ikram, 2016). The bands at 3289 and 3353 cm−1 were attributed to NH and OH stretching vibration, respectively. After oxidation of CS, the shifted bands of NH and OH stretching vibration and the decreased peak intensity of C-O-C indicated obvious changes of glucoside rings. There is a new peak at 1630 cm−1, implying the formation of aldehyde groups (Chen et al., 2023). In addition, 1H NMR spectra of CS and DCS are shown in Fig. 1d and e, respectively. The CS exhibited main signals at 2.52, 3.25–3.06, and 4.21 ppm, associated with H2, H3-H6, and H1, respectively (Luan et al., 2018). In 1H NMR spectrum of DCS, new signals appeared at 8.48 (H2), 8.20 (H3), and 5.28 ppm, attributed to aldehyde groups of C2 and C3, and hemiacetalic proton (Chen et al., 2023). Moreover, XPS was applied to further investigate the detailed change of chemical composition after the oxidation of CS, and O 1 s deconvolution peaks exhibited two sub-peaks including O1 (CO, 530.6–531.2 eV) and O2 (C-OH/C-O-C, 532.6–532.8 eV). As presented in Fig. 1f and g, the O1 peak area increased from 21.73 % to 27.32 % after oxidation of CS, further confirming aldehyde groups formation. The above FTIR, 1H NMR, and XPS analyses confirmed that the DCS was synthesized successfully. The molecular weight of CS and DCS was performed by GPC (Fig. 1h and Table 2 ). The peak molecular weight (M p), number-average molecular weight (M n), and weight-average molecular weight (M w) values of DCS were 4669, 3262, and 5177 g/mol (Table 2), respectively, which did not exhibit a significant reduction in molecular weight compared with CS. Additionally, the polydispersity index (PD) of CS and DCS were 1.40 and 1.59, respectively, demonstrating that the molecular weight distribution was relatively stable after oxidation. This was because the periodate can selectively oxidize the C2 and C3 position of chitosan (Wang et al., 2020).

Fig. 1i shows TG curves to investigate the thermal stability of CS and DCS. The thermal degradation of CS and DCS could exhibit three stages (room temperature-200 °C, 200–350 °C, and 350–600 °C), assigning to the loss of water molecules, the degradation of pyranose rings, and degradation of the skeleton structure, respectively. The residual mass of CS and DCS were 35.7 and 27.3 %, respectively, implying that the thermal stability of DCS was reduced after the oxidation reaction.

Given that CDs with high water dispersibility can strongly interact with nanofillers via non-covalent interactions. CDs containing large amounts of amino groups was prepared using CA and PEI, which allowed them to firmly immobilize on the SiO2 surface via electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds to synthesize CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids (Scheme 3 ). The satisfactory dispersion stability and compatibility of resultant CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids are the key to the uniform dispersion in the SM matrix and improving the adhesives' performances.

The morphologies of CDs, SiO2, and CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids were performed by TEM. It could be seen from Fig. 2a that the resultant CDs were well dispersed and exhibited spherical shapes with amorphous features. The HR-TEM image of CDs (inset of CDs in Fig. 2a) showed that the size of lattice fringe was 0.21 nm, demonstrating that the CDs had a distinct crystallinity. As displayed in Fig. 2b, the fabricated CDs had a particle size in the narrow range of 1.8–2.8 nm. For the TEM result of the CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids, it was clearly observed that a large amount of spherical small CDs was adsorbed on the SiO2 surface, which could result from the hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions between CDs and SiO2.

The CDs showed two characteristic peaks in UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2c). A peak centered at 224 nm was associated with the π-π* transition of CC conjugated system. The peak at 344 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition of CO from the CDs (Cailotto et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2021). After introduction of SiO2, the characteristic peaks of the n-π* transition of CO shifted to 338 nm, suggesting successful construction of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids. In addition, the synthesized CDs suspension displayed bright blue fluorescence irradiated by UV 365 nm, while the CDs@SiO2 suspension showed blue-green fluorescence (Fig. 2d). Meanwhile, no obvious precipitation occurred in CDs@SiO2 aqueous solution after standing for 2 d, but the dispersion of SiO2 in water was poor (Fig. S1). These phenomena affirmed that CDs could be well anchored on the surface of SiO2 via non-covalent interactions, thereby conferring CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids with good functionalization and dispersion. More importantly, the superb dispersion stability of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids in water played an important role in their uniform dispersion in SM-based adhesive systems.

Zeta potential measurement was applied to confirm the existence of electrostatic interactions in CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids. As depicted in Fig. 2e, SiO2 presented a negatively charged surface with a zeta potential of −12 mV, ascribing to the ionization of hydroxyl groups on the SiO2. In contrast, CDs nanoparticles were positively charged (11.1 mV), owing to the existence of amino groups. The CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids displayed a positively charged surface (9.83 mV), demonstrating that the presence of electrostatic interactions can enhance the combination of SiO2 with CDs.

The FTIR, XPS, and XRD spectra were employed to characterize the chemical compositions of CDs, SiO2, and CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids. As presented in Fig. 2f, peaks at 1552 and 1650 cm−1 were related to CC and NH vibrations, respectively, revealing the skeleton structure of CDs (Cailotto et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2019). The peaks at 3271 and 1456 cm−1 were associated with the vibration of O-H/N-H and CN, respectively, implying that the amino groups and amide groups were formed on CDs surface. The peak at 1152–1012 cm−1 (CO stretching) predicted the presence of oxygen-containing groups on CDs. Additionally, the bands of SiO2 at 802, 975, and 1055 cm−1 were associated with SiO, Si-OH, and Si-O-Si vibration, respectively (Xu et al., 2022). For CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids, a band at 3271 cm−1 shifted to 3264 cm−1, implying the hydrogen bonds interaction between CDs and SiO2. The main characteristic peaks of CN and NH of CDs occurred and shifted to 1463 and 1644 cm−1, respectively. Moreover, the Si-OH peak at 975 cm−1 nearly disappeared, and the peaks shift of Si-O-Si and SiO from 1055 and 802 cm−1 for SiO2 to 1052 and 784 cm−1 for CDs@SiO2, respectively. The above results demonstrated the successful synthesis of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids, which was also supported by the XPS analysis. It could be seen from Fig. 2g that after SiO2 incorporation, the Si 2s and Si 2p peaks of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids appeared, and their peak intensities decreased compared with SiO2. Meanwhile, O 1s deconvolution peak of CDs contained O1 (CO, 530.1–530.2 eV) and O2 (C-OH/C-O-C, 531.3–531.6 eV) (Fig. 2h). For CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids, the O2 peak area increased from 61.48 % to 64.46 % compared with CDs, which was caused by the existence of SiO groups. The CDs exhibited a broad peak at 19.8° (Fig. 2i), revealing CDs contained amorphous carbon. The SiO2 had a broadened diffraction peak at 22.7°. It was noticed that the diffraction peak of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids occurred at 21.5°, with the interlayer spacing (d) of 4.22 nm, confirming that the crystallinity of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids was affected by the physical interactions between SiO2 and CDs.

TGA measurement was applied to investigate the thermal stability of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids (Fig. 2j). It could be seen that the residual mass of CDs and SiO2 were 0.98 and 88.94 %, respectively. This observation implied that the CDs were less thermally stable after heating at 600 °C. While the residual mass of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids was 30.18 %, proving improved thermal stability of the synthesized CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids. The multifunctional nanoparticles prepared by non-covalent interactions are suitable for promoting the functionality of the materials such as high reactivity, thermal stability, and multifunctionality.

The manufacturing process of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive is depicted in Scheme 4 . The unique compositions and structure of the oyster are of great interest since the soft organic components can provide binding sites, while the hard inorganic parts contribute to strong cohesion. The prerequisite of inorganic components reinforcement is stable dispersion and superior compatibility. Moreover, the strong interface interactions between the organic polymeric matrix and inorganic nanofillers are the key to the regulation of adhesive's performance. Herein, the prepared CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids contained amino groups, which can provide more active sites and improve the dispersion of SiO2 in organic SM matrix. DCS as a glue molecule can promote robust interface interactions of CDs@SiO2 with SM matrix, thereby tightly connecting CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids and organic SM matrix via the Schiff base reaction. The construction of biomimetic organic-inorganic structure could be expected to confer soy protein adhesive with high performance benefiting from the rigid structure and strong cohesion effect (electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bonds, reversible imine bonds, and nonreversible covalent bonds).

The structural basis of protein adhesives was elucidated by FTIR (Fig. 3a). The main characteristic peaks of soy protein occurred at 1642, 1528, and 1238 cm−1, associated with the amide I (CO stretching), amide II (NH bending), and amide III (NH and CN stretching), respectively (Li et al., 2019; Zeng, Yang, et al., 2021). The free hydroxyl and amino groups stretching vibrations were observed at 3274 cm−1. The band at 3274 cm−1 in pure SM adhesive shifted to a higher wavenumber in modified SM-based adhesives and the peak intensities at 1642, 1528, and 1233 cm−1 significantly decreased, demonstrating strong hydrogen bonds interactions. Additionally, the peak intensity of NH stretching decreased after modification, revealing that the crosslinking reaction of DCS with CDs@SiO2 and SM matrix happened. The reduced peak intensity at 1392 cm−1 was observed due to the crosslinked structure formation limiting the side chain COO- (1392 cm−1) symmetric stretching of protein molecules (Li et al., 2019). To investigate the crosslinking reaction of modified adhesives, the C 1s deconvolution peaks of various adhesives were performed by XPS (Fig. 3b–e). C 1s peak of the SM and MSM adhesives included C1 (C-C/C-H, 284.8 eV), C2 (C-O/C-N, 286.2–286.4 eV), and C3 (CO, 287.6–287.9 eV). From Fig. 3b and c, the C2 peak area increased from 24.11 % to 50.41 % in MSM adhesive, implying that the ring-opening reaction of EGDE with amino groups of SM occurred. After introduction of DCS, an enhancement in C2 peak area was observed in comparison with MSM adhesive, meaning the imine bonds (C=N) were formed between amino groups of SM and aldehyde groups of DCS (Yuan et al., 2021). For MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive, a reduction in C3 peak area revealed that aldehyde groups of DCS were consumed by introducing CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids. These phenomena demonstrated that DCS could crosslink CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids and organic SM matrix via the Schiff base reaction. Moreover, the Si 2p curves of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive (Fig. 3f) displayed two sub-peaks of Si-O-Si and SiC. The existence of SiC implied the superb compatibility between CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids and organic SM matrix.

13C NMR could be employed to further confirm the crosslinked structure of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive (Fig. 3g). The SM adhesive exhibited the main signals at 40.2, 157.3, and 172.7 ppm, relating to β-C, arginine C, and amino acid carbonyls, respectively. The signals at 115.7 and 72.5 ppm were associated with aromatic C of histidine and functional groups in carbohydrates, respectively (Kakalis & Baianu, 1990; Lépine et al., 2015; Li et al., 2019). For the modified SM-based adhesives, the reduced peak intensities at 172.7, 157.3, 115.7, and 40.2 ppm revealed the reactive groups of EGDE, DCS, and CDs@SiO2 can react with functional groups of amino acids. Moreover, a signal intensity at 72.5 ppm decreased, indicating that the polysaccharide components in the SM matrix can effectively participate in the crosslinking reaction. These changes suggested that the intermolecular chemical and physical interactions in biomimetic organic-inorganic MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive occurred.

The crystallinity of the SM-based adhesive system was performed by XRD (Fig. 3h). The typical diffraction peaks of SM adhesive were located at 2θ ≈ 9.0° and 20.0°, belonging to α-helix and β-fold of soy protein secondary structure, respectively (Zhao et al., 2021). After introducing DCS and CDs@SiO2, the peak of α-helix disappeared. The peak of β-fold was right shift and the peak intensity weakened compared with the pristine SM and MSM adhesives. These occurrences can be explained that the dense crosslinking structures formed by introduction of DCS and CDs@SiO2 could disrupt the protein ordered arrangement, thus destroying the original protein crystal structure. This outcome is consistent with FTIR, XPS, and 13C NMR analyses.

TGA measurement was applied to investigate the thermal stability of different adhesive samples (Fig. 3i). Three stages of thermal degradation of SM-based adhesives could be observed, which were the loss of water molecules (room temperature-200 °C), degradation of small molecules and unstable side chains (200–260 °C), and degradation of protein skeleton structure (260–600 °C). The maximum degradation peak temperature of pristine SM adhesive in T2max was 302 °C. The T2max value of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive increased to 312 °C. Moreover, the residual mass of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive was 31.4 % after heating at 600 °C, which was much higher than that of pure SM adhesive (24.2 %) (Table S1). These outcomes indicated that introducing DCS and CDs@SiO2 into the SM matrix to generate an organic-inorganic structure was conducive to the thermal stability of modified protein adhesives.

The production of plywood needs not only superior cold-pressing strength between wood substrates, but also outstanding water-resistant adhesion of the fabricated plywood. The former can ensure that the pre-assembled wood substrates were not dislocated during the transportation before hot pressing while the latter is a crucial indicator of the plywood performance for interior use. Fig. 4a presents the lap shear strength of the adhesives to assess the cold-pressing properties between wood substrates. The adhesion strengths of the pristine SM and MSM adhesives were 0.21 and 0.27 MPa, respectively. The poor bonding strength was caused by the loose internal structure of soy protein as well as the presence of water layer between wood veneer and adhesives. The shear strength of the adhesive enhanced to 0.47 MPa with the addition of DCS. After incorporation of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids, the cold-pressing strength of the adhesives remarkably increased, with a maximum of 0.74 MPa, which was 252 % higher than that of SM adhesive. This enhanced cold-pressing strength was likely associated with the entanglement of DCS and SM chains with wood substrate, and the synergistic effects of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids and DCS to effectively facilitate strong interactions with SM matrix, yielding stronger adhesion.

Fig. 4b presents the wet shear strength of three-ply plywood manufactured from various adhesives. Pristine SM adhesive hardly exhibited any wet adhesion strength because the weak hydrogen bonds between protein molecules were easily destroyed by water molecules. For MSM adhesive, the introduction of EGDE can increase the adhesion strength due to the ring-opening reaction of EGDE with SM. Notably, the introduction of DCS formed crosslinking network with protein, which contributed to generation of mechanical riveting between the adhesive and plywood, thus efficaciously increasing the wet strength of the adhesive. The addition of SiO2 had little effect on the adhesive's wet strength, primarily because of the poor interface compatibility of SiO2 with the SM matrix. Surprisingly, the wet shear strength of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesives effectively enhanced and can be optimized by adjusting the mass ratio of CDs to SiO2. A maximum wet shear strength of 1.36 MPa was achieved with a 1:2 mass ratio of CDs to SiO2, an 84 % higher than that of MSM adhesive, which was far beyond the Type II indoor-use plywood requirement (≥0.7 MPa). This water-resistant adhesion benefited from the biomimetic structure and robust interface interactions (hydrogen bonds, covalent bonds, and electrostatic interactions), thereby efficiently blocking the ingress of water molecules in a wet environment. The superior water resistance of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive was further confirmed by the residual rates (RD) and moisture absorption rate (MA) results, as presented in Fig. 4c. The RD and MA of SM adhesive were 66 and 36 %, respectively, owing to soy protein's inherent spherical structure and hydrophilicity (Vakhtang et al., 2002; Zeng et al., 2022). The MSM and MSM/DCS adhesives had a higher RD and a lower MA than the SM adhesive, illustrating enhanced water resistance. It was worth noting that incorporating CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids into protein adhesive displayed the highest RD of 73 % and lowest MA of 31 %. This outcome verified that being similar to the oyster adhesive mechanism, introduction of DCS and CDs@SiO2 enhanced interactions with the organic protein matrix and effectively inhibited water penetration.

Generally, the adhesives' debonding work is positively correlated with toughness (Mazzotta et al., 2020; Narayanan et al., 2020). Fig. 4d illustrates the force-distance curves and the adhesives' debonding work based on wet strength tests. It was noted that the debonding work of the SM and MSM adhesives were 0 and 0.33 J, respectively, showing an extremely limited toughness. Strikingly, introducing DCS and CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids into the protein matrix improved the adhesives' toughness. The debonding work of the MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive reached 0.80 J, which was a 146 % increase in comparison with MSM adhesive. The surface morphology of the cured adhesive layer was applied to further confirm the superior toughness of the MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive. It can be seen from Fig. 4e that the SM and MSM adhesives were nonuniformly coated and appeared some cracks on the glass slide, confirming the inherent brittleness and weak cohesion strength of soy protein adhesives. It was observed that a uniform and tight adhesive layer of the cured MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive validated the distinctly enhanced toughness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The reinforcement mechanism of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive
ce:section.ce:para: To gain insights into the reinforcement mechanism of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive, the cross-sectional and surface morphologies of the different cured adhesives were performed by SEM and AFM, respectively. The surface morphologies of the adhesives are illustrated in Fig. 5a. The root-mean-square roughness (Rq) of SM adhesive was 27.5 nm, demonstrating a flat and smooth surface. This phenomenon resulted from the weak hydrogen bonds interaction and tight agglomeration of protein molecular chains during curing (Chen, Lyu, et al., 2022; Chen, Pizzi, et al., 2022; Li, Jin, et al., 2022). By contrast, the Rq of the MSM adhesive increased substantially to 46.6 nm, which benefited from the enhanced crosslinking structure between the EGDE and SM matrix. After the incorporation of DCS and CDs@SiO2, the Rq of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive rapidly increased to 73.7 nm, implying a denser and rougher surface. This result revealed that DCS could facilitate strong interface interactions of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids with protein matrix and increase the crosslinking density of organic-inorganic hybrid adhesive.

SEM was conducted to observe the cross-sectional morphologies of the adhesives (Fig. 5b and c), which largely determined the adhesives' adhesion performance. The SM and MSM adhesives showed a loose surface with many brittle cracks and gas holes, illustrating that the adhesives exhibited brittle fracture when the external force was applied. The MSM/DCS adhesive showed a dense surface with fewer small cracks, implying that the aldehyde groups of DCS reacted with amino groups of SM to generate a compact crosslinking network. It should be remarked that the incorporation of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids led to the anchoring effects in the protein adhesive, and the prepared MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive displayed a rough and dense surface with numerous protuberances. The dense structure contributed to the remarkably enhanced water resistance of the adhesives. Additionally, the C, N, O, and Si elements derived from MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive were evenly dispersed in the protein matrix (Figs. 4c and S2), illustrating the well-dispersed CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids were conducive to the interface interactions with the adhesives. The introduction of DCS can facilitate robust interface interactions (hydrogen bonds, electrostatic forces, and covalent bonds) of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids with protein matrix to form a denser crosslinking structure. The rigid skeleton structure and multiple interactions in the adhesive gave rise to the strong cohesive and interfacial bonding strength, which significantly improved the adhesion property of plywood. Wood fracture surfaces exhibited pulling fibers after the plywood that used the MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive was measured by wet shear strength test, while the pure SM adhesive with smooth fracture showed a cohesive failure (Fig. S3). The proposed strengthening and toughening mechanisms of the MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive are exhibited in Scheme 5 . Being similar to oyster system, the CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids were uniformly dispersed and provided a large rigid skeleton support for the SM matrix. DCS as a glue molecule tightly connected the whole polymer/inorganic network. When the protein adhesive was subjected to a mechanical load, DCS as a dynamically-connected bridges of organic-inorganic phases can transfer the stress to CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids, which provided an efficient energy dissipation to prevent stress concentration. Therefore, this unique biomimetic oyster system with robust interface interactions and rigid structure was conducive to the improvement in strength, water resistance, and toughness of protein adhesives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mildew resistance and antibacterial properties of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that soybean meal adhesive containing protein and polysaccharides is susceptible to bacteria and fungi, which results in a shortened shelf life. Therefore, the ability of protein adhesive to maintain antimildew and antibacterial activities is the key to expanding the application of the adhesive. Freshly formulated and cured SM-based adhesives were stored at 30 °C and 99 % RH to observe the fungal growth (Fig. 6a and S4). Mildew occurred on the surface of liquid SM adhesive after 1 d while the liquid MSM adhesive did not show mold until 14 d, implying improved mildew resistance of MSM adhesive. The liquid MSM/DCS and MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesives had no mildew after 30 d, demonstrating outstanding antimildew activity due to the presence of DCS. Additionally, both cured SM and MSM adhesives displayed mildew within 10 d (Fig. S4), indicating that the protein adhesives through chemical crosslinking had limited mildew resistance. No mildew appeared for the cured MSM/DCS and MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesives after 30 d. This was because the formation of Schiff base structure after curing improved the antimildew properties of the adhesives (Bavya et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020).

Both gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) and gram-positive (S. aureus) were chosen to test the adhesives' antibacterial activities, and the results are shown in Fig. 6b and c. SM adhesive did not show inhibition zones due to its poor antibacterial activities. With the addition of EGDE, the inhibition zone was 5 mm against E. coli, but no inhibition zone was found against S. aureus, implying relatively weak antibacterial abilities. It was worth noting that the obtained MSM/DCS adhesive displayed superior antibacterial properties against S. aureus and E. coli, showing inhibition zones of 4 mm against S. aureus and 15 mm against E. coli. After incorporation of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids, inhibition zones of the resultant adhesive against S. aureus and E. coli did not improve significantly, implying the presence of DCS conferred protein adhesives with antibacterial activities. The aldehyde groups could irreversibly destroy cell walls and cytoplasmic membranes of bacteria and the positively charged amino groups were adsorbed on bacteria surfaces via electrostatic interaction, which synergistically inhibited bacterial growth (Ren et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2017). Moreover, the variation in inhibition zones against S. aureus and E. coli might be attributed to structural differences between Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria (Li, Thakur, et al., 2018). These outcomes indicated that DCS played a pivotal role in promoting antimildew and antibacterial activities in protein adhesives (Fig. 6d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Durability and fluorescent properties of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2 adhesive
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to bonding wood substrate, the MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2–2 adhesive is also suitable for bonding other substrates, such as steel, glass, and Al, and the results of their lap shear strength are displayed in Fig. 7a. As expected, the resultant adhesive showed outstanding adhesion to different substrates surface. This was because the multiple interactions between active groups of the adhesive and hydroxyl groups or metal ions on the substrate surface brought extremely high cohesion and created a huge spatial position lock to prevent its slippage. Additionally, the wood and steel substrates withstood a weight of 500 g in water to assess the long-term water resistance stability, which was a critical factor for practical application (Fig. 7b). Of note, the steel and wood substrates could still lift a weight of 500 g without detachment of the adhesive after being soaked in water for 49 d, demonstrating stably long-term water resistance. This superior durability was attributed to the construction of biomimetic oyster organic-inorganic adhesives that made strong adhesive contact with substrates and maintained excellent resistance to water, even in long-term water environments. Therefore, the MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2-2 adhesive offers powerfulness toward potential practical applications.

The fluorescent characteristic of a product can prompt its application in anti-counterfeiting and information transmission, which realizes the personalized and visual detection of the materials. As presented in Fig. 7c, the construction of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids endowed soy protein adhesive with stronger fluorescent properties than the pure SM adhesive. The different substrates including poplar, glass, steel, and Al, bonded with MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2-2 adhesive exhibited blue-green fluorescence irradiated by UV 365 nm (Fig. 7d), which resulted from the unique fluorescence of CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids. Most strikingly, the prepared MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2-2 film also displayed strong blue-green fluorescence irradiated by UV 365 nm, and can be conveniently processed into fluorescent materials in various shapes such as star and heart (Fig. 7e). Overall, incorporating CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids into soy protein matrix maintained strong fluorescence intensity in both adhesive and film, which exhibits the potentials for anti-counterfeiting and information transmission.

The performances (including high cold-pressing strength, wet shear strength, mildew resistance, fluorescent properties, and long-term water resistance) of MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2-2 adhesive were compared with some other bio-based adhesives (Fig. 7f and Table S2). The performances of fabricated MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2-2 adhesive were superior to those of bio-based adhesives. Besides, the cost of this biomimetic adhesive is calculated, and the composition details are shown in Table S3. The price of resultant adhesive is around 2839 RMB (or 408 US dollar)/ton, which is cheaper than those of commercial soy protein-based adhesives (approximately 4000–4500 RMB/ton or 575–647 US dollar/ton) (Chen, Lyu, et al., 2022; Chen, Pizzi, et al., 2022), holding great promise for industrial use. Therefore, introducing DCS and CDs@SiO2 into the protein matrix to build an oyster-inspired organic-inorganic adhesive can develop an economical and durable functional adhesive.

Inspired by the oyster organic-inorganic hybrid structure, a novel multifunctional soy protein adhesive with high bonding strength, long-term water resistance, excellent toughness and antimildew properties was prepared by introducing DCS and CDs@SiO2 into SM matrix. The synthesized CDs@SiO2 nanohybrids exhibited the improved interface compatibility and dispersion in the SM matrix, and DCS containing aldehyde groups as a dynamically-connected glue molecule promoted strong and stable interface interactions between CDs@SiO2 and SM via the Schiff base reaction. The highly reactive aldehyde groups of DCS and excellent dispersion stability of CDs@SiO2 greatly improved the adhesion strength, toughness and long-term water resistance of the adhesive. The results showed that the MSM/DCS/CDs@SiO2-2 adhesive exhibited superior cold-pressing strength (0.74 MPa), water-resistant strength (1.36 MPa), long-term water resistance (49 d), and toughness (0.80 J). The resultant bioinspired structure also imparted the adhesive with good adhesion to other substrates, such as steel, glass, and Al. In addition, the modified adhesive displayed outstanding antimildew and antibacterial properties due to the presence of DCS. The fluorescent properties of the adhesive and film could further broaden the application of protein-based materials for realizing security testing. Therefore, this study provides an alternative new strategy for fabricating a high-strength, antimildew, and tough adhesive, possessing great potential for adhesion in industrial and household applications.
